content.assessment: Finland's 2010 funding reform, which shifted toward census-based allocation and coincided with reduced full-time special-education placement, offers a strong, if not fully causal, precedent for the incentive-based logic underlying TP-live-t4. This lens finding directly supports the proposal's core mechanism: removing diagnosis-contingent fiscal triggers is associated with reduced over-identification pressure in at least one comprehensive national system.
opportunities: Finland's precedent could be used to justify piloting census-based elements in Hungary's financing architecture with built-in evaluation to test whether the same association holds
strengths: Direct documented association between Finland's shift to census-based funding and reduced full-time special-education placement supports the proposal's central causal theory
Finland's case demonstrates census-based systems as a viable alternative architecture to diagnosis-gated financing at national scale
threats: Context fit is a genuine limitation: Finland's municipal fiscal structure, demographic scale, and administrative capacity differ from Hungary's, so the funding-identification relationship observed in Finland may not replicate under Hungary's distinct incentive and governance context
weaknesses: The Finnish evidence is correlational at the system level and does not establish micro-level causal mechanisms transferable to Hungary's specific multiplier structure

Political durability of such a shift in Finland is not addressed in the supplied evidence, leaving open whether census-based reform faced or would face comparable resistance in a different administrative culture
